aliases: Dang Gui Duck Rice 当归烤鸭饭

english: Dang Gui Duck Rice 当归烤鸭饭

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Dang Gui Duck Rice; mapped to duck dish as the closest defensible parent class. Dang Gui (Angelica sinensis) herbal component and rice base are not individually represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cooked rice dishes with meat — composite rice-based dish with roasted duck and herbal broth
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (rice-based composite dish) used as closest defensible anchor. No specific code exists for Dang Gui Duck Rice in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Dang Gui Duck Rice or sufficiently close herbal duck rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Dang Gui Duck Rice typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a serving of steamed white rice (approximately 200 g) topped with two to three pieces of roasted duck (approximately 120 g) and herbal dang gui gravy (approximately 30 g).

nutrient_source.title: Dang Gui Duck Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Dang Gui Duck Rice (当归烤鸭饭), cr_id G00010694. All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical entry. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central roasted duck composition (USDA SR Legacy #05140) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dang Gui Duck Rice as a composite dish. GI of 64 is estimated using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate contributor (GI ~64 for white rice, University of Sydney GI Database, glucose reference). Carbohydrate per serving = 25.19 g/100g × 350 g serving = 88.2 g available carbohydrate; however, dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g moderates glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as (64 × 35.8 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 22.9. Available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate minus dietary fibre: (25.19 − 3.7) × 3.5 = 75.3 g; GL = (64 × 75.3) / 100 ≈ 48.2 for full plate; per 100 g GL = (64 × 21.49) / 100 ≈ 13.8. Reported GL per serving (350 g plate) = 22.9 using standard portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g (Na:K ratio < 1)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced duck gravy to limit sodium intake, as a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio provides partial mitigation but total meal sodium should be monitored when consuming alongside other sauces or condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load from white rice base (GI ~64, GL ~22.9 per 350 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce portion size to lower the glycaemic load, and should remove duck skin to reduce fat-driven insulin resistance; the 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre provides modest glycaemic moderation but is insufficient to offset the high carbohydrate load of a standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~1.4 g/100 g from duck skin and roasting; sodium 231 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should remove duck skin before eating to reduce saturated fat intake, request less gravy to limit sodium, and moderate portion frequency given that high-temperature roasting generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which may contribute to vascular inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dang Gui (Angelica sinensis) anti-inflammatory properties; low saturated fat when skin removed
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dang Gui has documented anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that may benefit rheumatoid arthritis patients; however, duck skin should be removed to minimise saturated fat intake, and consumption frequency should be moderated due to AGE generation from high-temperature roasting.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dang Gui Duck Rice (当归烤鸭饭) is a Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of crispy roasted duck served over steamed white rice, accompanied by a fragrant herbal broth made with dang gui (Angelica sinensis), ginger, garlic, and soy-based seasonings. The duck is roasted at high temperature to achieve a lacquered, crispy skin and tender, juicy meat, then sliced and placed atop a mound of steamed white rice. The dish is finished with a ladling of the herbal dang gui gravy, which imparts a distinctive earthy, slightly sweet, and warming flavour profile characteristic of traditional Chinese herbal cooking. Dang gui is a well-known herb in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) used for its purported blood-nourishing and anti-inflammatory properties. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is notably high at 3.7 g/100 g for a rice dish, contributed by the herbal components. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g, which is borderline for hypertension management. The dish has a medium glycaemic index (~64) driven by the white rice base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore hawker centres and is associated with Chinese culinary heritage. The dish is not suitable for halal diets without certification and contains soy and sesame allergens.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010694, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA SR Legacy roasted duck data as HPB does not separately report these fractions. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice analogue as no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.6 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.7 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #05140 (Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted) proportionally adjusted for the rice-dominant composition of this dish; HPB Foodo does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry. 2. Trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 g/100 g: confirmed negligible for roasted duck and rice-based dishes per USDA SR Legacy data. 3. Cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 mg/100 g in the HPB source; this is noted as potentially underreported for a duck-containing dish — USDA SR Legacy reports ~77 mg/100 g for roasted duck meat and skin. The HPB value is preserved as the canonical source value but flagged for review. 4. Fields set to -1 (data not available from approved sources): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed vitamin, mineral, and moisture data for this specific composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 5. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement exists for Dang Gui Duck Rice as a composite dish. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; duck rice dishes in Singapore hawker centres may or may not be halal-certified depending on the stall.
